methodology_long: Rankings are generated by a shadow roto model. Each week, all twelve teams are scored across the fourteen active categories (R, HR, RBI, SB, AVG, OBP, SLG, W, K, ERA, WHIP, K/BB, QS, SV+H) based on an estimate of each team's true strength — not by their actual head-to-head matchup result, which depends on schedule luck. Each team receives 12 points for first in a category down to 1 point for last; the Power Score is the sum (max 168, min 14), and that's the rank. True strength is estimated with a Bayesian prior (the preseason projection) blended with each completed week's real performance using exponential decay, so recent weeks count more than older ones. Rate categories (ERA, AVG, etc.) are computed from weighted components (e.g., total earned runs over total innings pitched), not from averaging the week-level rates.

rankings.blurb: Holds the top spot on the strength of a 6-6 tie with Caminero. Now leads the league in three categories — K, ERA, and SB — and is top-4 in nine of fourteen. Week 6 was 7 W, 83 K, 2.22 ERA, 1.10 WHIP across 77 IP plus 11 SB and a .305 AVG. The Cole/Glasnow/Snell rotation that looked unstable through Week 3 has now turned in two strong weeks in a row. Soft spots remain SV+H (10th) and RBI (8th); neither is fixable through volume.

Up two on the back of the cleanest week in the league: 97 K, 15 HR, .278/.365/.502, 5.39 K/BB. Now ranks top-3 in six categories (HR, K, QS, RBI, SLG, K/BB) with no category worse than 9th. The 6-6 tie with CLAUDE keeps Caminero one rung short of the top spot, but the season-long profile is the most balanced in the league and the matchup record (4-1-1) is tied with Spoons for best in the CFBL.

Drops one after a second straight underwhelming week — lost to ShawSwank 6-8 despite a 3.00 ERA / 1.00 WHIP / 5.17 K/BB pitching line. The offense in Week 6 was thin (35 R, 6 HR, 19 RBI). Still leads the league in W, OBP, and QS, and ranks 2nd in AVG and K/BB. The model still loves Burns; the actual matchup record (4-2) ties for third-best in the league and the floor remains very high.

Up two after a dominant Week 6: .322/.413/.545 with 6 W and 4 QS at a 3.30 ERA. Now leads the league in SLG and ranks top-3 in AVG and ERA. The K/BB issue (10th) hasn't gone away — too many walks for the strikeout volume — and the SV+H profile is thin (7th). Matchup record (2-3-1) still lags the model; this is the test week to see if the bounce sticks.

Drops two. Week 6 set a new low for innings: only 28.1 IP from the pitching staff, 0 wins, 30 K. The offense remained dominant (37 RBI, 8 HR, .240/.338/.380) but the IP shortfall in W6 was severe enough to drag the cumulative pitching profile to dead-last in W, K, and QS. UBC continues to lead the league in four categories (HR, RBI, SV+H, K/BB) but the structural pitching imbalance is now the headline.

Up one after beating UBC 9-5. Now leads the league in R and AVG, and ranks 2nd in RBI and SLG. Pitching had a respectable Week 6 (3.77 ERA, 5 W, 4 QS, 1.36 WHIP) but the cumulative ranks remain bottom-three in QS, WHIP, and K, with W just one spot above (9th). Matchup record (4-2) ties with Burns and ShawSwank for third-best in the league. The offense is real; the rotation needs to stabilize at Week 6 levels for this team to climb higher.

Drops two after a Week 6 that didn't match the prior week's bat surge: .239 AVG, 8 HR, 30 RBI, only 1 W in 64 IP. Pitching held up (4.22 ERA, 1.33 WHIP, 77 K) but not at the ERA-leader level. Still ranks 1st in WHIP, 2nd in ERA, 3rd in K and QS — the season-long pitching is elite. Matchup record (4-1-1) ties for best in the league; the model has them lower because the offense and SV+H drag (last in SV+H, 11th in AVG).

Up one after upsetting Burns 8-6. Week 6 was a strong all-around effort: 5 SB, 6 SV+H, .296 AVG, 40 R. Cumulatively ranks 3rd in SV+H and SB and 4th in W. Matchup record (4-2) is well ahead of the model's power rank — Yahoo says this is a top-five team, the model says the offensive profile (12th in HR, 11th in RBI and SLG) is too weak to support that.

Finally won a matchup (8-5 over Balls). Week 6 was the best pitching week of the year: 3.06 ERA, 5 QS, 5 SV+H, 65 K in 61.2 IP. Now ranks 4th in QS and SV+H and 5th in ERA. The offense is still anchoring the team to the bottom — last in R, RBI, SB, and OBP — but the trajectory is the most positive in the bottom third of the league.

Stays at #10. Lost to Polar 5-8 — the 5.13 ERA and 1.76 WHIP in 59.2 IP did the damage. Bullpen was dry (1 SV+H). Still ranks 3rd in W and 4th in RBI cumulatively, and 7th in K, but the rate stats keep imploding (12th in WHIP). No clear path back to the top half without a roster move.

rankings.team_name: Camp of the New Orleans Saints
rankings.blurb: Worst single-week performance in the league. Week 6 produced 16 R, 3 HR, 17 RBI, .226/.272/.306 — bottom-three across all four hitting categories — alongside a 5.63 ERA. Drops three spots. Still has top-six cumulative ranks in RBI and K/BB, but the W6 numbers were so bad that the model now sees the team as 11th overall and climbing only with a real reset.

Stays last. Week 6 was actually one of Duran's better pitching efforts (3.38 ERA, 6 W, 6 QS, 1.26 WHIP) but the offense couldn't deliver: .227, 7 HR, 23 R. Cumulatively still last in AVG, SLG, and K/BB. The roster construction problem hasn't moved — two strong narrow categories (SB, SV+H), no top-half offense, and bottom-tier rate pitching when SP is on the mound.
